evidence: Survey catalogs log-based anomaly detection techniques (OASIS, Jia et al., LogSed) as a major category
Survey catalogs distributed tracing-based techniques for both anomaly detection and RCA as the most common instrumentation approach
Survey covers causality graph-based RCA methods including MonitorRank, MicroHECL, Aggarwal et al., and CloudRanger
Survey finds most techniques address detection or RCA in isolation; only TraceAnomaly and Seer provide integrated pipelines
Survey identifies ML model accuracy degradation under service evolution as a key challenge requiring costly retraining
Survey finds no surveyed technique provides explainability or countermeasure recommendation
Industry interviews identify distributed tracing as key solution (S3) for holistic observability, providing bird's eye view of request flow
Paper empirically documents the gap between distributed system complexity and monitoring capability through 28 industry interviews
Most interview participants consider culture and mindset challenges more difficult than technical challenges
Participants note that trace ID propagation across independently developed services is not consistently assured
Practitioners acknowledge AI potential but note preconditions (data quality, context propagation, central storage) are still missing
Industry interview study provides practitioner perspective complementing the technical survey; confirms organizational challenges that technical solutions alone cannot address
Introduces intermediate fusion of traces, logs, and KPIs via GLU gating for microservice diagnostics
Extends log-based AD with Hawkes process modeling of event occurrences as part of multi-modal fusion
Uses trace latency time series and dependency graphs extracted from traces as core inputs
Extends graph-based RCA by applying GAT on service dependency graphs with multi-modal node features
Demonstrates empirically that pipeline gap causes noisy labels (FOR 0.63-0.83) degrading RCL; end-to-end approach closes the gap
Multi-source fusion achieves F1=0.988 AD and HR@1=0.982 RCL, vastly outperforming trace-only baselines
Joint multi-task learning outperforms separate AD+RCL pipeline approaches on both benchmark datasets
Addresses the survey's identified gap of independent AD and RCA techniques by proposing the first end-to-end multi-source framework
Core paper for graph-based RCA using BFS propagation chain traversal with type-specific anomaly detection
Introduces anomaly propagation chain analysis with type-specific detection and correlation-based pruning
Pruning with threshold 0.7 reduces time 39% while maintaining HR@3=0.67 on 75 Alibaba incidents
Type-specific models (RF, OC-SVM, rules) achieve HR@3=0.67 vs 0.40/0.49 for uniform approaches
Linear execution time scaling across 28 Alibaba subsystems (7-1687 services)
Integrates type-specific detection into graph traversal pipeline, partially bridging the AD-RCA gap
Anomaly propagation chain analysis is a specific traversal strategy within the broader causality-graph RCA framework
Introduces Multivariate BOCPD for anomaly detection in microservices
Proposes graph-free alternative (RobustScorer) that outperforms graph-based RCA methods
Leverages failure propagation chain assumption for anomaly detection timing
RobustScorer with median/IQR outperforms mean/std baselines across 3 benchmarks
Multivariate BOCPD achieves best F1 (0.75-0.82) vs univariate baselines (0.05-0.72)
Demonstrates pipeline gap: combining existing AD+RCA methods yields inconsistent results; end-to-end approach solves this
Robust modular design (separate AD + RCA) achieves competitive performance without joint training
CIRCA extends graph-based RCA with formal causal inference: intervention recognition criterion on CBN, structural graph from architecture, regression-based hypothesis testing
CIRCA's descendant adjustment encodes anomaly propagation logic: scores propagated from descendants to ancestors to prefer actionable root causes over propagation intermediaries

CIRCA proves intervention recognition requires Layer 2 causal knowledge; CBN provides bridge from observational to interventional; Intervention Recognition Criterion is provably necessary and sufficient
RHT conditions anomaly scoring on parent metrics in CBN via regression, avoiding propagation false positives. CIRCA (RHT+DA) achieves AC@1=0.404 vs NSigma 0.323 on Oracle dataset
Structural graph (from architecture + TSEL meta-metrics) outperforms PC-gauss, PC-gsq, PCTS for DFS and CIRCA scoring on Oracle database dataset
CIRCA extends MicroHECL's DFS-MH graph traversal approach with formal causal inference framework and regression-based scoring, addressing MicroHECL's heuristic correlation assumptions
BARO outperforms CIRCA on 3 microservice benchmarks using nonparametric methods without causal graph, challenging CIRCA's claim that CBN-based intervention recognition is necessary
Defines and validates fault visibility, fault coverage, and OFO metrics on OpenTelemetry Astronomy Shop demo; OFO measured at 2/3 baseline and 3/3 after config change
Experiments show design alt B (5% sampling) achieves same OFO as C (10%) at lower cost (3.05% vs 5.33% CPU overhead), and alt A improves FC but not OFO
OXN framework extends chaos engineering with instrumentation treatments; YAML-based reproducible experiments covering 3 fault types × 3 design alternatives with 10 repetitions each
Introduces and validates the fault visibility, fault coverage, and OFO metric framework
Defines observability experiment formalism (SUE, workload, treatments, response variables) and implements it in OXN
Uses distributed traces as response variables; demonstrates trace sampling rate impact on fault visibility
Fault visibility metrics are the core quantitative foundation of the observability engineering methodology
Observability experiments are the primary methodology for systematic observability design assessment
Formalizes Distributed Tracing as a design pattern including tail-based sampling, OpenTelemetry adoption, span propagation mechanics
Completes the 11-pattern cloud-native monitoring catalog with Distributed Tracing, Application Metrics, Infrastructure Metrics patterns
Formalizes Application Metrics as a design pattern covering Four Golden Signals, RED, READS, push/pull collection, cardinality management
Formalizes Infrastructure Metrics as a design pattern covering USE method, autoscaling triggers, SLA compliance, sampling resolution trade-offs
Eleven monitoring design patterns validated through industry known-use cases; pattern format provides reusable, communicable engineering guidance
Conceptual argument and Netflix known-use case supporting tail-based over head-based sampling for retaining anomalous traces
Four Golden Signals, USE method, RED/READS frameworks formalized as metric selection guidance; THRON and GumGum industry use cases
Monitoring design pattern catalog directly addresses the observability engineering tooling research direction
Application metrics is a core pillar of observability foundations alongside tracing and logging
Infrastructure metrics are a foundational observability concern for distributed systems resource management
OTel is the primary instrumentation mechanism for distributed traces in Application Insights
Application Insights collects metrics via OTel signals (RED/four golden signals patterns)
Application Insights features (app map, live metrics, availability) implement core monitoring design patterns
OTel primer defines spans and traces as the core distributed tracing vocabulary; explains trace_id/span_id structure
OTel primer defines metrics as one of three telemetry signals; covers gauges, counters, histograms
OTel primer defines SLI and SLO as core observability vocabulary for measuring reliability
Original source of Four Golden Signals (Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation) — the canonical metric selection framework
Introduces error budgets and SLO-based reliability thinking; symptom vs cause alerting framework
Four Golden Signals are the primary evidence for structured metric selection frameworks improving monitoring
Ch. 6 covers trace structure (trace_id, span_id, parent_span_id), B3 propagation, waterfall visualization; defines traces as stitched structured events
Ch. 5 defines structured events as the foundational observability building block; argues metrics are lossy compressed events
Ch. 12 argues event-based SLIs detect partial failures missed by threshold monitoring; Honeycomb case study
Ch. 11 defines ODD practice; instrumentation-as-PR-requirement; glass castle anti-pattern
Ch. 17 provides comprehensive sampling taxonomy; argues tail-based sampling preserves anomalous traces despite higher collector overhead
Honeycomb case study: SLO burn rate detected brownout while all threshold monitors showed green
Symptom vs cause monitoring argument; white-box vs black-box monitoring distinction supports SLO-based approach
